rejectionReason: Your app uses a third-party payment system (Stripe) for digital content that must use In-App Purchase.
whatDeveloperDid: Integrated Stripe checkout to sell premium digital features like custom themes, advanced filters, and exclusive content within the app. Digital goods and services consumed within the app must use Apple's In-App Purchase system.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 3.1.1 - Business - Payments - In-App Purchase: Your app uses a third-party payment mechanism (Stripe) for digital content, features, or services that are consumed within the app. Per App Store Review Guidelines, apps offering digital goods or services for consumption within the app must use In-App Purchase. Please implement StoreKit for all digital content purchases.
fix: Replaced Stripe checkout with Apple's StoreKit 2 for all digital goods purchases. Stripe was kept only for physical goods/services not consumed within the app (if applicable). Implemented IAP products in App Store Connect for all premium features.
behavioralSignals: credit card form for premium features

rejectionReason: Your app does not include a mechanism to restore previously purchased subscriptions.
whatDeveloperDid: Implemented a subscription paywall but did not include a 'Restore Purchases' button. Users who reinstalled the app or switched devices had no way to restore their active subscriptions without re-purchasing.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 3.1.2 - Business - Payments - Subscriptions: Your app does not include a 'Restore Purchases' mechanism. Apps that offer auto-renewable subscriptions must include a restore mechanism to allow users to restore their previously purchased subscriptions on a new device or after reinstalling the app.
fix: Added a clearly visible 'Restore Purchases' button on the subscription/paywall screen. Implemented the SKPaymentQueue.restoreCompletedTransactions() (or StoreKit 2 equivalent) to restore active subscriptions. Also added a restore option in the app settings.

rejectionReason: Your app does not clearly display auto-renewable subscription terms before the purchase is initiated.
whatDeveloperDid: Displayed a subscription paywall with pricing and a 'Subscribe' button but did not include the required auto-renewal terms, such as the subscription duration, price per period, and that the subscription automatically renews unless cancelled at least 24 hours before the end of the current period.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 3.1.2 - Business - Payments - Subscriptions: Your app does not clearly display the following required subscription information before the user completes the purchase: the price and duration of the subscription, that payment will be charged to the iTunes Account, that the subscription automatically renews unless auto-renew is turned off at least 24 hours before the end of the current period, and links to your Privacy Policy and Terms of Use.
fix: Added all required subscription terms directly on the paywall screen in legible text. Included: price per period, subscription duration, auto-renewal disclosure, cancellation instructions, and links to Privacy Policy and Terms of Use. The terms are displayed before the subscribe button so users see them before purchasing.

rejectionReason: Your app contains a link that directs users to an external payment mechanism for digital content.
whatDeveloperDid: Included a button or link in the app labeled 'Upgrade to Premium' that opened the developer's website pricing page where users could purchase premium digital features via Stripe or PayPal, bypassing Apple's IAP system.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 3.1.1 - Business - Payments - In-App Purchase: Your app includes a link that directs users to an external mechanism for purchases that should be made through In-App Purchase. Specifically, tapping 'Upgrade to Premium' opens a web page where users can purchase premium features. Apps may not include buttons, external links, or other calls to action that direct users to purchasing mechanisms other than IAP for digital content.
fix: Removed the external payment link entirely. Replaced the web-based upgrade flow with StoreKit In-App Purchase. If eligible for the External Link Account Entitlement (reader apps, music streaming, etc.), applied for the entitlement with proper commission arrangements.

rejectionReason: Your In-App Purchase products failed to load during review.
whatDeveloperDid: IAP products worked correctly in the sandbox testing environment but failed to load during App Review. The products were not in the correct status in App Store Connect (they were still 'Waiting for Review' or 'Missing Metadata'), causing StoreKit to return empty product arrays.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 3.1.1 - Business - Payments - In-App Purchase: We were unable to purchase the In-App Purchase products in your app. When we attempted to view your subscription options, no products were displayed. Please ensure your IAP products are properly configured and in the correct status.
fix: Ensured all IAP products were in 'Ready to Submit' status in App Store Connect before submitting the app binary. Verified product IDs matched exactly between the app code and App Store Connect. Submitted the IAP products for review alongside the app binary. Added error handling UI to show a message and retry button when products fail to load.

rejectionReason: The subscription price displayed in your app does not match the price configured in App Store Connect.
whatDeveloperDid: Hardcoded the subscription price as '$4.99/month' in the app UI, but the actual price in App Store Connect was set to $9.99/month. The mismatch was caught during review when the reviewer compared the displayed price with the StoreKit payment sheet price.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 3.1.2 - Business - Payments - Subscriptions: The subscription price displayed in your app ($4.99/month) does not match the price set in App Store Connect ($9.99/month). The pricing information displayed to users must accurately reflect the actual purchase price.
fix: Removed all hardcoded prices from the app UI. Instead, used StoreKit's Product.displayPrice property to dynamically fetch and display the current price from App Store Connect. This also ensures correct pricing is shown across different currencies and regions.
behavioralSignals: different price in UI vs payment sheet
price doesn't match App Store Connect

rejectionReason: Your app offers no functionality without a paid subscription and the free trial provides no usable experience.
whatDeveloperDid: Created an app where all content was behind a paywall. The app offered a '7-day free trial' but immediately required payment information and locked users into a subscription. There was no lite/free tier to explore the app's functionality before committing to a purchase.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 3.1.2 - Business - Payments - Subscriptions: Your app locks users into a subscription immediately with no way to experience the app's core functionality before purchasing. Apps should provide some level of access or a meaningful free experience so users can evaluate the app before subscribing.
fix: Added a limited free tier that allows users to access basic features without subscribing. Users can create up to 3 items, view content with ads, and explore the app's core functionality. The premium subscription removes limits and ads. Also ensured the free trial doesn't require payment information upfront.

rejectionReason: Your app offers randomized virtual items for purchase (loot boxes) without disclosing the probability of receiving each type of item.
whatDeveloperDid: Built a mobile game with gacha/loot box mechanics where players could purchase virtual item packs with random contents using IAP, but did not disclose the drop rates or probability of receiving each rarity tier of items.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 3.1.1 - Business - Payments - In-App Purchase: Your app offers loot boxes or other mechanisms that provide randomized virtual items for purchase but does not disclose the odds of receiving each type of item to the user prior to purchase. Apps offering such mechanisms must include the probability of each type of item before the user commits to the purchase.
fix: Added a 'Drop Rates' information button next to every loot box purchase option. The button displays a clear breakdown of probabilities for each item rarity tier (e.g., Common: 60%, Rare: 25%, Epic: 10%, Legendary: 5%). This information is shown before the purchase confirmation.

rejectionReason: Your app uses Stripe for a tipping/donation feature for digital content creators, which must use In-App Purchase.
whatDeveloperDid: Askaloo (a Q&A platform) implemented a tipping feature using Stripe where users could tip content creators directly via credit card payments within the app, bypassing Apple's IAP system for what Apple classified as digital content consumption.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 3.1.1 - Business - Payments - In-App Purchase: Your app uses a third-party payment mechanism (Stripe) for tips/donations to content creators. Tips for digital content or services consumed within the app are considered digital goods and must use In-App Purchase. Please implement IAP for all tipping functionality.
fix: Replaced Stripe tipping with Apple's In-App Purchase consumable items for tip amounts. Created IAP products for different tip tiers ($1, $5, $10, $25). While Apple takes its commission on IAP transactions, this was the only way to keep the tipping feature in the app.

rejectionReason: Your app appears to be a white-label or template app that is not sufficiently differentiated.
whatDeveloperDid: Submitted a payment processing app that was a white-labeled version of a payment platform SDK. Multiple nearly identical apps from different business accounts used the same codebase with only branding differences (logo, colors, business name).
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 3.2.2 - Business - Other Business Model Issues - Unacceptable: Your app is a white-label version of another app. We have identified multiple apps with the same binary that differ only in branding. Creating multiple apps from a template or app generation service is considered spam and is not permitted on the App Store.
fix: Instead of publishing separate white-label apps for each business, consolidated into a single app with multi-tenant support. Each business gets their own branded experience within the single app through configuration. Alternatively, if each business has significantly unique features, ensured each version has meaningful functional differences beyond just branding.
behavioralSignals: multiple similar apps from same codebase

rejectionReason: Hardcoded pricing in your app does not match the prices configured in App Store Connect.
whatDeveloperDid: Displayed hardcoded pricing strings throughout the app (e.g., 'Premium: $2.99/mo') that were set during development. When the pricing was later changed in App Store Connect to $4.99/mo, the hardcoded strings were not updated, creating a price mismatch.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 3.1.2 - Business - Payments - Subscriptions: We noticed that the pricing displayed in your app does not match the actual subscription pricing in App Store Connect. Your app shows '$2.99/month' but the configured price is '$4.99/month'. Please ensure pricing accuracy.
fix: Replaced all hardcoded price strings with dynamic pricing fetched from StoreKit. Used Product.displayPrice to show localized, accurate pricing that automatically reflects the current App Store Connect configuration and the user's local currency.

rejectionReason: Your subscription products are still in review and cannot be tested.
whatDeveloperDid: Submitted an app update that referenced new IAP subscription products, but the products were submitted separately and were still pending review. The app binary was approved but the subscriptions remained in 'Waiting for Review' status for weeks, preventing the update from being published.
whatAppleSaid: Your app has been approved, but the In-App Purchase products associated with this version are still in review. Your app update cannot be released until all associated IAP products are also approved. Please wait for the IAP review to complete.
fix: For future submissions, ensured IAP products were submitted and approved before or simultaneously with the app binary. Contacted App Review via the Resolution Center to expedite the IAP review. Used the 'Submit with next app update' option in App Store Connect to link IAP products to the app submission.

rejectionReason: Your In-App Purchase product metadata language does not match the primary language of your app.
whatDeveloperDid: Configured IAP product names and descriptions in English in App Store Connect, but the app's primary language and storefront was set to Japanese. The IAP purchase sheet displayed English text in a Japanese-language app, creating an inconsistent user experience.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 3.1.1 - Business - Payments - In-App Purchase: Your In-App Purchase product names and descriptions are in English, but your app's primary language is Japanese. Please localize your IAP product metadata to match the primary language of your app for each storefront where it is available.
fix: Added Japanese localizations for all IAP product names and descriptions in App Store Connect. Used the App Store Connect localization features to provide product metadata in all languages the app supports.

rejectionReason: Your app includes a link to an external purchase mechanism in an error message.
whatDeveloperDid: When a user hit a feature limit, the app displayed an error message saying 'You've reached the free limit. Upgrade at https://example.com/pricing to unlock unlimited access.' This URL led to a web checkout page with Stripe payment, bypassing IAP.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 3.1.1 - Business - Payments - In-App Purchase: Your app directs users to an external purchase mechanism in an error message. Specifically, when reaching a feature limit, the app displays a URL to your website's pricing page. Apps may not direct users to external purchase mechanisms for digital content, even in error messages or alerts.
fix: Removed the external URL from the error message. Replaced it with a button that opens the in-app subscription/purchase screen powered by StoreKit IAP. The error message now reads 'You've reached the free limit. Tap Upgrade to unlock unlimited access.' with an IAP-powered upgrade flow.

rejectionReason: Your app uses PayPal to sell premium digital features that must use In-App Purchase.
whatDeveloperDid: Integrated PayPal checkout to sell premium digital features like ad removal, additional storage, and advanced editing tools within a photo editing app.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 3.1.1 - Business - Payments - In-App Purchase: Your app uses PayPal as a payment mechanism for digital content and features consumed within the app. Specifically, the 'Remove Ads' and 'Pro Editing Tools' purchases use PayPal checkout. Digital goods must be purchased through In-App Purchase.
fix: Removed PayPal integration for all digital goods. Implemented StoreKit In-App Purchase for the 'Remove Ads' (non-consumable) and 'Pro Tools' (subscription) products. PayPal can only be kept for physical goods or services delivered outside the app.

rejectionReason: Your app's business model has changed significantly, triggering a full re-review.
whatDeveloperDid: An app that was previously approved as a free utility added a subscription paywall in an update that locked core features behind payment. Apple flagged this as a significant business model change and conducted a thorough re-review, questioning whether the app provided enough value to justify the subscription.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 3.2 - Business - Other Business Model Issues: Your app has undergone a significant business model change from a free utility to a paid subscription service. We need to re-evaluate whether the app's functionality justifies the subscription pricing and whether existing users are adequately served by the new model.
fix: Grandfathered existing users into the premium features they previously had for free. Added a meaningful free tier with core functionality intact. Ensured the subscription provided clear additional value beyond what was previously free. Provided detailed notes in the resolution center explaining the business model change.

rejectionReason: Your app charges users for iCloud storage sync which is a system-level capability.
whatDeveloperDid: Created a notes app that locked iCloud sync behind a paid subscription. While the app stored data locally for free, accessing your own data across devices via iCloud required purchasing the premium subscription.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 3.1.3 - Business - Payments: Your app charges users for iCloud sync functionality, which leverages system-level capabilities. You should not create a purchase to gate access to Apple system features that users expect as standard functionality.
fix: Made basic iCloud sync available to all users for free. Restructured the subscription to provide value through advanced features (collaboration, advanced organization, export options) rather than gating system-level sync functionality.

rejectionReason: Your app charges for access to built-in iOS capabilities.
whatDeveloperDid: Built an app that charged a subscription fee to enable features that are essentially wrappers around built-in iOS capabilities like the flashlight, compass, and level tool, without adding meaningful additional value.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 3.2.2 - Business - Other Business Model Issues: Your app charges users for functionality that duplicates built-in iOS capabilities (flashlight, compass, level) without providing sufficient additional value or unique features. Apps should not monetize basic system functionality.
fix: Added significant unique value beyond the system capabilities: custom lighting modes, measurement history with export, AR-based measurements, and professional calibration tools. The subscription was repositioned around these genuinely unique features rather than the basic system functionality wrappers.

rejectionReason: Your app does not include instructions for how to manage or cancel a subscription.
whatDeveloperDid: Implemented subscription IAP but did not include any information in the app about how users can manage their subscription, view renewal dates, or cancel through iOS Settings.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 3.1.2 - Business - Payments - Subscriptions: Your app does not include information about how to manage subscriptions. You must provide a link or instructions within the app that direct users to their subscription management settings where they can manage or cancel their subscription.
fix: Added a 'Manage Subscription' button in the app settings that deep-links to the iOS subscription management page (using UIApplication.open(URL(string: 'https://apps.apple.com/account/subscriptions')!)). Also added text explaining how to cancel and when the next renewal date is.
behavioralSignals: no way to manage subscription in app

rejectionReason: Your app requires a subscription with no way to use the app without payment.
whatDeveloperDid: Launched an app with a hard paywall that showed the subscription screen immediately on launch with no dismiss button. Users could not access any part of the app without purchasing a subscription first, with no free trial or lite functionality.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 3.1.2 - Business - Payments - Subscriptions: Your app requires a subscription purchase to access any content or functionality, with no free tier, trial, or sample content available. Users should be able to evaluate the app and experience some functionality before being required to purchase.
fix: Added a dismissible paywall with a 'Continue with Free' option. Created a free tier with limited but meaningful functionality (3 sessions per day, basic features). Added a 7-day free trial option for the full premium experience. The paywall is shown after the user has had a chance to experience the app.
behavioralSignals: paywall on launch with no dismiss
